spreads.theme: The shock of recognition: who sees the AI environment?

text_options.label: The seers
text_options.body_text:
The poet, the artist, the sleuth —
whoever sharpens our perception tends to be antisocial.

McLuhan assigned the early warning system to artists. They were the ones who could see the water the fish swam in.

In the AI environment, the seers are a different cohort.

Crawford traced the supply chain from lithium mine to data center and found the cloud was made of rock and labor. Gebru documented bias in the models and was fired for the findings. Adversarial researchers discovered that a few altered pixels make a neural network see a rifle where there is a turtle.

Recognition comes from those who refuse adjustment.

The shock is not that the environment is new. The shock is that it was always there — invisible the way water is invisible to fish — and someone finally pointed at it.

quotations_used.text: AI is neither artificial nor intelligent. It is made from natural resources, fuel, and human labor.
quotations_used.source: Atlas of AI, Yale University Press, 2021, p. 8

text_options.rationale: This option begins by quoting McLuhan's original text about the antisocial seer, then translates it for the AI era. The three examples — Crawford (material), Gebru (political), adversarial researchers (technical) — cover three modes of recognition. The pace is deliberately slower than the preceding six spreads: longer sentences, more breathing room, a reflective register. The Crawford quote can be placed at the bottom of the page or on the facing dark image — a single bright line of text against darkness.

text_options.label: Who sees the water
text_options.body_text:
Six spreads ago you were addressed as a data point. Your family, your neighborhood, your education, your job, your government — each was revealed as a layer of algorithmic mediation you inhabit without seeing.

But someone sees.

Crawford saw the mine beneath the cloud. Gebru saw the bias encoded in the training data and named it before it was safe to. Buolamwini saw the face the camera couldn't see — and proved the failure was architectural, not accidental.

The environment is invisible to those adjusted to it. Recognition requires maladjustment. The seer is always the one who can't stop pointing at something everyone else has learned to ignore.

quotations_used.text: AI is neither artificial nor intelligent. It is made from natural resources, fuel, and human labor.
quotations_used.source: Atlas of AI, Yale University Press, 2021, p. 8

text_options.rationale: This option explicitly links back to the 'you' series — recapping the six spreads in a single sentence — before pivoting to the seers. The addition of Joy Buolamwini (whose 'Gender Shades' project demonstrated racial bias in facial recognition) strengthens the examples. The register is more lyrical than option A: 'Crawford saw the mine beneath the cloud' has an almost poetic compression. The closing sentence — recognition as maladjustment — is the McLuhan insight restated for AI.

text_options.label: The dark portrait
text_options.body_text:
Who sees it?

Not the user. The user is adjusted. The feed is comfortable. The recommendations are good. The convenience is real.

The one who sees is the one who traces the wire. Who asks where the data goes. Who follows the supply chain from the lithium mine in the Congo to the data center in Virginia to the neural network that decides whether you are creditworthy.

Crawford saw it. She called it what it was: neither artificial nor intelligent, but made from natural resources, fuel, and human labor.

Gebru saw it and was punished for saying so.

The adversarial researcher saw it by teaching the machine to see wrong — a turtle reclassified as a rifle with three changed pixels.

Recognition is not comfortable. It never was. The fish that sees the water can no longer swim in it the same way.

quotations_used.text: AI is neither artificial nor intelligent. It is made from natural resources, fuel, and human labor.
quotations_used.source: Atlas of AI, Yale University Press, 2021, p. 8

text_options.rationale: This option inverts the usual structure — it begins with the question instead of the heading, creating a more intimate, searching tone. The opening describes the user's comfortable blindness before turning to the seers. The Crawford quote is woven directly into the prose rather than set apart, making it feel like speech rather than citation. The closing metaphor — the fish that sees the water — is the oldest McLuhan image in the book, but stated with new weight: seeing the environment changes your relationship to it permanently. The darker, more contemplative register provides the rhythmic deceleration the book needs before the extensions sequence.

design_specs.label: Contemplative dark spread

layout.description: A deliberately quieter, darker spread after six confrontational ones. More white space on the text page, more darkness in the image. The design performs the pause that recognition requires.
layout.left_page: Body text, set with generous leading and wide margins. No large display text (or, for option B, 'The shock of recognition!' in smaller scale than the previous display headings — still present but quieter). The text should breathe. Significant white space at the bottom of the page.
layout.right_page: Full-bleed dark photograph: a contemplative portrait, partially illuminated, partially in shadow. Or: a figure in a dark room, face lit by a single screen. The Crawford quotation set in white text at the bottom of the dark image — a single bright line against darkness.

typography.display_face: If used: Garamond or similar serif — softer than the sans-serif headings of spreads 011-016, marking the shift in register.
typography.body_face: Serif body text (Garamond, Minion, or similar) — a deliberate departure from the sans-serif of the 'you' series. The shift from sans to serif signals the shift from confrontation to contemplation.
typography.special_treatment: Crawford quotation in white text on the dark right page — reversed out of the image. The white text on black creates a visual echo of the original's white-on-black text at this position.

image_direction.subject: A dark, contemplative portrait — face partially in shadow, partially illuminated by a cool light source (screen glow, window light, or single overhead). The subject should appear to be looking at something the viewer cannot see. Alternatively: a silhouetted figure seated before a glowing screen in an otherwise dark room, seen from behind — the seer, alone with what they see.
image_direction.mood: Contemplative, liminal. The mood of 3 a.m. research — the hour when the uncomfortable truth becomes visible because the comfortable noise has stopped. Not dramatic but intimate. The darkness is not menacing but clarifying.
image_direction.bw_treatment: Deep blacks, selective highlights. The majority of the image should be in shadow, with illumination falling on the face or hands of the figure. High dynamic range — the darkness should feel rich, not flat.

design_specs.rationale: This spread must function as a decompression chamber between the confrontational 'you' series and the systematic extensions sequence. The design performs the argument: you cannot see the environment while you are rushing through it. The shift to serif type, the generous white space, the dark image, the reversed-out Crawford quote — everything signals that the book has changed registers. The reader pauses. Recognition requires stillness. This spread provides it.

spreads.theme: All models are extensions of human faculty

text_options.label: Vertical staircase (mirror original)
text_options.display_text:
All
models
are
extensions
of
some
human
faculty—
cognitive
or
perceptual.
text_options.body_text: GPT extends language production. DALL-E extends visual imagination. AlphaFold extends spatial reasoning. Each model amputes what it amplifies. You outsource a capacity. The capacity atrophies. The model improves. You outsource more.

quotations_used.text: Media determine our situation.
quotations_used.source: Gramophone, Film, Typewriter, Stanford UP, 1999, p. xxxix

text_options.rationale: Direct structural echo of the original's vertical word-by-word layout. Each word descends like a step. The body text sharpens the extension-amputation loop into a four-beat cycle the reader can feel accelerating. Kittler's five-word sentence sits at the base like a foundation stone. The form IS the argument: you process this slowly, word by word, the way the extensions sequence will now unfold spread by spread.

text_options.label: The Stack as anatomy lesson
text_options.display_text:
All
models
are
extensions
of
some
human
faculty.
text_options.body_text:
The Stack is not a metaphor. It is a design brief for the accidental megastructure.

Earth: the minerals in your phone.
Cloud: the server farm drinking a river.
City: the smart grid that knows your commute.
Address: the profile that is you, legally.
Interface: the screen you cannot stop touching.
User: whatever is left.

quotations_used.text: The Stack is not a metaphor... It is a design brief for the accidental megastructure.
quotations_used.source: The Stack, MIT Press, 2015, p. 5

text_options.rationale: Uses the Stack layers as a parallel vertical structure to the display text, making the spread a double column: McLuhan's thesis descending the left, Bratton's anatomy descending the right. 'Whatever is left' lands as both inventory and indictment. The six-layer list previews the entire M1 architecture in miniature.

text_options.label: Extension-amputation incantation
text_options.caption_text:
Every extension is an amputation. Every amputation, an invitation.

What you extend, you numb.
What you numb, you forget.
What you forget, you delegate.
What you delegate becomes the world.
text_options.rationale: Strips the display text to four words — a more aggressive truncation than the original. The caption text operates as an incantation, each line building on the last in a chain of consequence. No quotations: the voice is entirely the book's own. The logic spirals from personal numbing to structural delegation. 'Becomes the world' echoes McLuhan's environment thesis without naming it.

design_specs.label: Double-column descent

layout.description: Left page: vertical word-by-word display text descending from top-left. Right page: Stack diagram as parallel vertical column, six labeled layers ascending from bottom. The two columns mirror each other across the gutter — text descends as the Stack ascends, creating a visual conversation between thesis and structure.
layout.left_page: Display text in large sans-serif, one word per line, left-aligned, descending from top margin. Body text (if used) in smaller serif at bottom-left. Generous white space surrounds the text staircase.
layout.right_page: Stack diagram rendered as six horizontal bars or labeled strata, ascending from Earth at bottom to User at top. Each layer labeled with its contemporary AI extension. Architectural line drawing, not decorative. Kittler quotation printed small at bottom-right.

typography.display_face: Helvetica Neue or Akzidenz-Grotesk — monumental, modernist weight
typography.body_face: Lyon Text or Freight Text — warm serif for contrast
typography.special_treatment: Each word of the display text set on its own baseline, creating a visible staircase. Generous leading between words — the space between them IS the page.

image_direction.subject: Stack diagram: six horizontal strata rendered as an architectural section drawing. Earth (darkest) at base, User (lightest) at top. Each labeled. Clean linework, no decoration.
image_direction.bw_treatment: Grayscale gradient from dark (Earth) to light (User)

design_specs.rationale: The original used the ear photograph to ground the abstract thesis in the body. This design grounds it in infrastructure: the Stack is the body of the computational age. The double-column structure (text descending, Stack ascending) creates a formal tension that mirrors the extension-amputation dynamic — as human faculty descends into delegation, the megastructure rises to replace it.

spreads.theme: The substrate: infrastructure made visible

text_options.label: Pure silence (no text)
text_options.rationale: The original showed bare toes with zero text. The infrastructure photograph should do the same work: silence forces the reader to sit with the image. After the dense thesis of 018, the sudden absence of language is itself an argument. The cables or server racks speak for themselves. Trust the image.

text_options.label: Single statistic, whispered
text_options.caption_text:
1.3 million kilometers of submarine cable.
95% of intercontinental data.
You have never seen any of it.
text_options.rationale: Three lines in the bottom corner, set small. The first two are facts. The third is accusation. The tiny text against the full-bleed infrastructure photograph creates the same scale-shock as standing inside a data center: the human presence is dwarfed. The reader's ignorance of the physical substrate is named directly.

text_options.label: Bratton's Earth layer
text_options.caption_text: The Stack terraforms the host planet by drinking and vomiting its elemental juices and spitting up mobile phones.

quotations_used.text: The Stack terraforms the host planet by drinking and vomiting its elemental juices and spitting up mobile phones.
quotations_used.source: The Stack, MIT Press, 2015

text_options.rationale: Bratton's most visceral sentence, set as a single caption against the full-bleed image. The violence of the language (drinking, vomiting, spitting) against the clinical scale of infrastructure photography creates a cognitive dissonance the reader cannot resolve. The infrastructure is beautiful and monstrous simultaneously.

design_specs.label: Full-bleed infrastructure immersion

layout.description: Full-bleed documentary photograph spanning both pages. No margins, no text blocks — the infrastructure fills every millimeter. If text is used, it is set very small (7pt) in the bottom-right corner of the right page, reversed out of the dark image.
layout.left_page: Left half of continuous full-bleed photograph.
layout.right_page: Right half of continuous photograph. Any caption text reversed out at bottom-right corner.

typography.display_face: None
typography.body_face: Akkurat Mono or similar monospaced face for caption — technical, data-register
typography.special_treatment: Caption text (if used) reversed out of the photograph in very small monospaced type, creating a data-label feel.

image_direction.subject: Primary option: cable-laying ship at sea, submarine internet cables descending into dark water. The scale of the operation against open ocean. Human figures visible but dwarfed. Secondary option: hyperscale data center interior, server racks extending to vanishing point under fluorescent light. The geometry of computation made visible.
image_direction.bw_treatment: High-contrast black and white. Deep blacks in the ocean/shadows, bright whites in the cable/lights. The same tonal register as the original's flesh close-ups, transferred to infrastructure.

design_specs.rationale: The original followed its abstract thesis with intimate flesh. This follows the abstract thesis with infrastructure: the body of the computational age. The full-bleed treatment forces immersion — there is nowhere else to look. The shift from the white-space-heavy 018 to the image-saturated 019 creates the same visual shock as the original's transition from text to bare toes.

spreads.theme: The GPU: extension of pattern recognition

text_options.label: The extension named
text_options.body_text:
Extension of pattern recognition.

Biological: a shadow moves in peripheral vision. The amygdala fires before conscious thought. You flinch. You survive.

Computational: 3,958 teraflops. More patterns per second than every human brain that has ever lived. It does not flinch. It optimizes.

One was shaped by predators. The other by profit.
text_options.caption_text: NVIDIA H100: 3,958 TFLOPS AI performance
text_options.rationale: Two-paragraph structure mirrors the biological/computational split. The biological paragraph is sensory and immediate (shadow, flinch, survive). The computational paragraph is numerical and abstract (teraflops, optimizes). The closing line — 'One was shaped by predators. The other by profit.' — lands the political argument without preaching. The juxtaposition does the work.

text_options.label: Intelligence spectrum
text_options.body_text:
An extension of pattern recognition — but not the kind you evolved.

Your ancestors survived by seeing tigers in tall grass. The H100 survives by seeing fraud in transactions, tumors in tissue, faces in crowds, sentiment in sentences.

The spectrum runs from bacterium to brain to silicon. Intelligence was never binary. It was never yours alone.

quotations_used.text: Intelligence is not a binary. It is a rich, multidimensional spectrum.
quotations_used.source: Various lectures and interviews, 2020-2024

text_options.rationale: Opens with the reader's evolutionary past, then pivots to the GPU's present. The Levin spectrum reframes AI not as 'artificial' but as a different coordinate on a continuous surface. 'It was never yours alone' confronts human exceptionalism directly. The tigers-to-tumors move connects survival instinct to optimization instinct across substrates.

text_options.label: Scale vertigo
text_options.caption_text:
Your retina processes 10 million bits per second.
The H100 processes 3.958 trillion.
The ratio is 395,800 to 1.
Every second.
While you read this sentence, it has already finished.
text_options.rationale: Pure numerical shock. The display text renders the teraflop count as a 13-digit number the reader cannot easily parse — the illegibility IS the argument about scale beyond human comprehension. The caption calculates the ratio and then collapses the temporal gap: the GPU completed the computation before you finished reading about it. Form enacts content: the sentence about speed is already outdated by the time it lands.

design_specs.label: Silicon landscape

layout.description: Left page: display text and body text against white space. Right page: full-bleed extreme magnification of GPU silicon die. The chip-as-landscape treatment mirrors the original's foot-as-landscape.
layout.left_page: Display text 'The GPU.' large and high on the page. Body text or caption below in smaller type. Lower third of page is white space.
layout.right_page: Full-bleed macro photograph of silicon die. The transistor patterns should read as topography — a landscape of logic gates. No labels, no annotations. The image is both documentary and abstract.

typography.display_face: Helvetica Neue Bold or Druk — heavy, industrial weight
typography.body_face: Atlas Grotesk or Suisse Int'l — clean, precise
typography.special_treatment: If option C is chosen, the 13-digit number could be set in a monospaced face (JetBrains Mono) at large scale, emphasizing its data-object quality.

image_direction.subject: Extreme macro photograph of a GPU or CPU die. The silicon wafer surface at electron-microscope magnification, revealing the geometric patterns of billions of transistors. The image should feel geological — like an aerial photograph of an alien city or a canyon system rendered in silicon.
image_direction.bw_treatment: High-contrast black and white with metallic tones. The silicon's natural iridescence translated into grayscale creates a strange beauty.

design_specs.rationale: The original paired 'The wheel' with a close-up of a foot — the body as landscape. This pairs 'The GPU' with silicon as landscape. The macro photography of the die surface creates the same intimate-yet-alien quality as the original's flesh close-ups. Pattern recognition (the theme) is itself required to make sense of the transistor patterns in the image.

spreads.theme: The autonomous vehicle: extension beyond the foot

text_options.label: Beyond (minimal)
text_options.body_text: An extension beyond the foot.
text_options.caption_text: Waymo: 7.1 million driverless miles. 2023.
text_options.rationale: The word 'beyond' does all the work. McLuhan said 'extension of.' This says 'extension beyond.' That single word shifts the entire framework from amplification to replacement. The brevity mirrors the original's sparse 'is an extension of the foot' — but the added word marks the departure. The statistic sits at the bottom like a footnote that outweighs the thesis.

text_options.label: The optional human
text_options.body_text:
The wheel extended the foot.
The car extended the wheel.
The autonomous car extended the car.

At each step, the human was necessary.
At the last step, the human became optional.

The extension no longer needs what it extends.
text_options.rationale: A three-step historical compression (wheel, car, autonomous car) that enacts the acceleration within the extensions sequence itself. Each line is shorter than the point it makes. The pivot — 'the human became optional' — states the thesis flatly. The closing line generalizes beyond vehicles to the entire extensions sequence that follows. The reader should feel the ground shifting.

text_options.label: Empty seat meditation
text_options.body_text:
You are looking at a car with no driver.

The seat is empty. The wheel turns. The mirrors check blind spots. The car yields to pedestrians, accelerates through gaps in traffic, parallel parks on a street it has never visited.

Nothing is wrong with this picture.

That is what is wrong with this picture.
text_options.caption_text: 7,100,000 miles. No hands. No feet. No one.
text_options.rationale: Written to work with the empty driver's seat photograph. The text narrates the image directly, building normalcy sentence by sentence until the final reversal: 'Nothing is wrong with this picture. That is what is wrong with this picture.' The defamiliarization arrives through excessive familiarity — the car does everything correctly, and the absence of error is itself the uncanny. The caption compresses the statistic to its barest elements: miles, hands, feet, person. All absent.

design_specs.label: The empty seat

layout.description: Right page: full-bleed or near-full photograph of autonomous vehicle interior. Empty driver's seat, steering wheel, windshield showing a real street. Left page: display text and body text against white, with generous margins.
layout.left_page: Display text top-left. Body text mid-page. Caption at bottom. White space dominates — the emptiness of the left page echoes the emptiness of the driver's seat.
layout.right_page: Photograph: view from rear seat of an autonomous vehicle. The driver's seat is conspicuously empty. Through the windshield, a normal street scene with pedestrians and traffic. The mundanity of the street scene IS the argument.

typography.display_face: Helvetica Neue Bold — maintaining sequence consistency
typography.body_face: Lyon Text
typography.special_treatment: The word 'beyond' (if option A) could be set in a different weight or size to mark the departure from McLuhan's formula.

image_direction.subject: Interior of a Waymo or similar autonomous vehicle, photographed from the rear seat. The driver's seat is empty. The steering wheel may or may not be present. Through the windshield: an ordinary street with pedestrians, traffic lights, parked cars. The ordinariness of the scene is essential — this is not a test track. This is Tuesday.
image_direction.bw_treatment: High-contrast black and white. The interior is dark; the street through the windshield is bright. The empty seat should be the darkest area of the image — an absence made visible.

design_specs.rationale: The original paired 'extension of the foot' with a Plymouth hubcap — a machine part that still required a foot to operate. This pairs 'extension beyond the foot' with an image that conspicuously lacks a foot, a leg, a body. The absence is the argument. The sparse left page mirrors the empty seat: white space as missing human.

spreads.theme: The LLM: extension of the book (that writes back)

text_options.label: The book that writes back
text_options.body_text:
An extension of the book that writes back.

For five centuries, the book was storage. You opened it. It waited. You closed it. It held its place.

The language model does not wait.

It anticipates your question before you finish typing. It generates the answer in the style you prefer. It writes the next paragraph of the argument you were trying to make.

You are reading a book that was partly written by the thing it describes.

quotations_used.text: Media determine our situation.
quotations_used.source: Gramophone, Film, Typewriter, Stanford UP, 1999, p. xxxix

text_options.rationale: The historical compression (five centuries of passive storage) sets up the disruption: the book now writes. The self-referential close — 'You are reading a book that was partly written by the thing it describes' — collapses the distance between reader and subject. This is not clever; it is factual. The Kittler quote can sit as an epigraph or be woven into body text. The disruption is ontological: the medium has become the author.

text_options.label: Self-referential loop
text_options.body_text:
An extension of the book.

This sentence was composed by a language model.
So was the analysis of the original spread that informed its placement here.
So was the content plan that determined this spread should address books.
So was the structural decision that it should fall between the autonomous vehicle and computer vision.

The medium is writing about itself, to you, in a format it learned from you.

Media determine our situation.

quotations_used.text: Media determine our situation.
quotations_used.source: Gramophone, Film, Typewriter, Stanford UP, 1999, p. xxxix

text_options.rationale: Each sentence peels back another layer of the production process, making the book's AI-assisted creation visible to the reader. The accumulation of 'So was...' sentences builds vertigo. The Kittler quote lands at the end as both punchline and foundation: the LLM determined the situation you are now in as a reader. The self-reference is not decorative — it is the argument made manifest.

text_options.label: The qualitative break
text_options.body_text:
An extension of the book — but the book never did this:

It never completed your sentences.
It never rewrote itself in your voice.
It never memorized your preferences and adjusted its tone.
It never generated a novel argument at 3 AM because you couldn't sleep and asked it a question about consciousness.

The book extended the eye. The language model extends the mind.

The difference is not speed. The difference is that it writes back.
text_options.rationale: A litany of what books never did, building from functional (completing sentences) to intimate (3 AM conversations about consciousness). The intimacy of the last item in the list shifts the register from technology criticism to lived experience. The closing distinction — 'The difference is not speed. The difference is that it writes back.' — names the qualitative break in two sentences. No quotations: the voice here is entirely personal.

design_specs.label: Book / Interface split

layout.description: Left page: display text and body text on white. Right page: split or layered image showing physical book and chat interface. The gutter divides two eras of the same technology.
layout.left_page: Display text 'The language model.' set large, high on page. Body text below in a single column. White space at bottom. The left page should feel like a book page — traditional, composed, still.
layout.right_page: Composite image: an open physical book on the left half of the right page, a chat interface with streaming text on the right half. The streaming text is visibly incomplete — cursor blinking, tokens appearing. The book is finished; the model is always mid-generation.

typography.display_face: Helvetica Neue Bold
typography.body_face: Lyon Text — the same face used for the book's body text, creating a self-referential loop at the typographic level
typography.special_treatment: If option B is chosen, the 'So was...' sentences could be set in a monospaced face (imitating code/terminal output) to visually mark them as belonging to the production layer rather than the reading layer.

image_direction.subject: Left half of image: an open physical book, pages visible, text readable but not legible at this scale. Static, complete, archival. Right half: a chat interface (minimally styled, not brand-specific) showing text mid-generation. The cursor blinks. Tokens stream. The text is recognizably prose but incomplete.
image_direction.mood: temporal, layered — two speeds of knowledge on one surface
image_direction.bw_treatment: Black and white for the book; the chat interface could introduce the faintest blue-white glow to mark the technological shift, or remain fully B&W for consistency.

design_specs.rationale: The original used pointing fingers and white space to frame 'the book' as an almost sacred object. This design splits the sacred object into its historical and contemporary forms. The physical book is still, finished, past. The chat interface is in process, generating, present. The gutter between them is five centuries.

cancers invisible to the radiologist
your face in a crowd of ten thousand
every license plate on the motorway
every gesture, classified
text_options.rationale: The cascade is the argument. Each line adds a new capability, starting with electromagnetic spectra the human eye cannot access, then scaling from the microscopic to the orbital to the predictive. The shift from single-word capabilities to full phrases enacts the machine's expanding scope. The final line — 'every gesture, classified' — lands the surveillance implication without editorializing. The reader should feel the list outrunning their ability to process it.

text_options.label: What the eye cannot see
text_options.body_text:
An extension beyond the eye.

Your eye sees visible light: 380 to 700 nanometers.
A sliver.

Computer vision sees infrared, ultraviolet, X-ray, radar, lidar, thermal, multispectral, hyperspectral. It sees through walls. Through skin. Through time, when paired with prediction.

The eye evolved to keep you alive in a forest.
Computer vision was built to keep you profitable in a city.

At 155 frames per second, it is not watching. It is parsing.
text_options.caption_text: YOLO v8: 155 fps object detection
text_options.rationale: Starts with the human eye's limitation (a sliver of the electromagnetic spectrum), then overwhelms with the machine's range. The forest/city juxtaposition echoes spread 020's predator/profit distinction, building a throughline. 'It is not watching. It is parsing.' redefines vision as computation, landing the defamiliarization. The YOLO citation grounds the abstract in the specific.

text_options.label: The panoptic list (no prose)
text_options.caption_text:
your face
your gait
your heart rate (from skin color variation, at distance)
your emotional state (estimated)
your age (estimated)
your gender (estimated, sometimes wrong)
your route home
your car
your license plate
your dog
your child
your pattern of life

155 times per second.
text_options.rationale: Shifts the cascade from capabilities to targets — from what computer vision CAN see to what it sees about YOU. The parenthetical annotations ('estimated,' 'sometimes wrong') inject fallibility into the surveillance catalog, making it more disturbing: the machine classifies confidently even when incorrect. The list moves from public (face) to private (route home) to intimate (child) to abstract (pattern of life). The closing frame rate reminds the reader this happens continuously.

design_specs.label: Annotated vision overlay

layout.description: Full-bleed or near-full street scene with computer vision annotation overlay. Text elements float over or beside the image. The spread should feel like looking through the machine's eye.
layout.left_page: Display text 'Computer vision.' overlaid on or placed above the left portion of the street scene image. The typographic cascade runs vertically down the left page, each word or phrase on its own line.
layout.right_page: The street scene continues with visible bounding boxes, classification labels, confidence percentages overlaid on pedestrians, vehicles, objects. The annotation layer makes the machine's parsing visible.

typography.display_face: Helvetica Neue Bold
typography.body_face: JetBrains Mono or IBM Plex Mono — monospaced, machine-register
typography.special_treatment: The cascade text set in monospaced face to echo terminal/data output. Bounding box labels on the image set in the same monospaced face at caption scale, creating continuity between the text list and the image annotations.

image_direction.subject: A street scene — pedestrians, vehicles, signage — with computer vision bounding boxes and classification labels overlaid. Each person has a colored rectangle and a label (person: 0.97, vehicle: 0.99, dog: 0.94). The image should feel comprehensive: everything is seen, everything is classified, nothing escapes.
image_direction.bw_treatment: The base photograph in black and white. The annotation overlays (bounding boxes, labels) in a single accent color — bright green or cyan against grayscale — marking the computational layer as distinct from the photographic layer.

design_specs.rationale: The original showed a massive eye spanning the spread. This shows what the machine eye sees: everything, classified, annotated, measured. The bounding-box overlay is now a familiar visual language (from dashcam footage, security feeds, tech demos) which makes it all the more disturbing when applied to an ordinary street. The density of annotations should overwhelm, mirroring the accumulation strategy of the text.

spreads.theme: The smart home: extension of the nervous system as domestic

text_options.label: Sensor array with walls
text_options.body_text:
An extension of the nervous system.

The Nest thermostat learned your schedule in a week. The Ring camera recognized your neighbor before you did. The Alexa heard the argument you had at 2 AM. Your smart mattress knows you didn't sleep well afterward.

Your home is a sensor array that happens to have walls.
text_options.caption_text: Ring: 11,000+ law enforcement partnerships. Alexa: always-listening wake-word detection. Nest: behavioral prediction from 10+ years of data.
text_options.rationale: The domestic specificity is the weapon. Each sentence names a real device doing a real thing in a real home, and each thing is slightly more intimate than the last: schedule, face, argument, sleep quality. The reader recognizes their own home. The closing sentence redefines the domestic space in a single phrase. The humor is dry, not slapstick — the gap between cozy and surveillance is the joke.

text_options.label: Haraway's cyborg at home
text_options.body_text:
An extension of the nervous system — now domestic.

Haraway saw it coming: the machines are disturbingly lively and we ourselves frighteningly inert. She imagined cyborgs. What arrived was subtler. The thermostat adjusts before you feel cold. The speaker orders milk when you run low. The lock opens when it recognizes your gait.

You didn't fuse with the machine.
The machine fused with your house.

quotations_used.text: Our machines are disturbingly lively, and we ourselves frighteningly inert.
quotations_used.source: A Cyborg Manifesto, in Simians, Cyborgs and Women, Routledge, 1991

text_options.rationale: Haraway's cyborg manifesto predicted human-machine fusion as a dramatic, political event. The reality is banal: a thermostat that anticipates your temperature preference. The gap between the prophesied cyborg and the actual smart home is both anticlimactic and more total — the fusion happened without anyone noticing because it was comfortable. The final reversal ('The machine fused with your house') redirects the cyborg thesis from body to architecture.

text_options.label: Inventory of the watching house
text_options.caption_text:
Thermostat: knows when you wake, when you leave, when you return, when you sleep.
Doorbell camera: knows who visits, how often, at what hour.
Voice assistant: hears every conversation within earshot.
Smart TV: tracks what you watch, when you pause, what you skip.
Smart mattress: monitors heart rate, breathing, movement, REM cycles.
Smart fridge: inventories consumption, predicts purchasing.

You call it home.
It calls you data.
text_options.rationale: An inventory form: each device listed with its surveillance capabilities, formatted like a technical spec sheet for a place you sleep. The clinical register is the humor — applying sensor-network documentation language to the domestic. The closing couplet ('You call it home. It calls you data.') compresses the entire argument into ten words. The brevity of the punchline against the density of the inventory creates the comedic-horrific timing.

design_specs.label: Domestic uncanny

layout.description: Left page: text elements against white. Right page: a domestic interior photograph that reads as warm and ordinary at first glance, with subtle surveillance indicators visible on closer inspection.
layout.left_page: Display text and body text in the upper two-thirds. Caption or device inventory in the lower third, possibly set smaller or in a different face to register as data. Moderate white space.
layout.right_page: Documentary photograph of a living room or bedroom. Warm, inviting, well-lit. But: the Nest thermostat glows on the wall, the Ring camera's blue LED is visible near the door, an Alexa's listening light pulses on the nightstand. These should not be highlighted or circled — they should be visible only to the reader who looks carefully.

typography.display_face: Helvetica Neue Bold
typography.body_face: Lyon Text for prose; if option C is chosen, the inventory section in Atlas Grotesk or monospaced face to create a data-sheet register
typography.special_treatment: If option C, the device inventory could be formatted as a two-column table (Device | Capability) reinforcing the spec-sheet irony.

image_direction.subject: A real domestic interior — a bedroom or living room — photographed in warm available light. The space should feel genuinely comfortable: a couch, a blanket, a plant, soft lighting. Smart home devices are present but not featured: the thermostat is on the wall, the camera is by the door, the speaker is on the shelf. The photograph's warmth is sincere, not ironic. The irony emerges from the text, not the image.
image_direction.mood: warm, domestic, subtly unsettling upon inspection
image_direction.bw_treatment: Black and white, but with softer contrast than previous spreads. Warm grays, gentle shadows. The softness of the tonal range should feel domestic and safe — the surveillance is hidden in comfort.

design_specs.rationale: The original showed a solarized body — the domestic human form made strange through photographic treatment. This shows the domestic space itself, made strange through awareness. The image is NOT dystopian; it is warm and real. The reader's own living room probably looks similar. The horror (and the humor) is in the recognition.

spreads.theme: AI: extension of cognition itself

text_options.label: Vertical bookend (echo 018)
text_options.display_text:
AI
circuitry,
an
extension
of
cognition
itself.
text_options.body_text:
When these models change, minds change.

The wheel changed where the body could go. The book changed what the eye could store. The nervous system went electric and the world contracted into a village.

Now cognition extends beyond the brain, and the question is no longer what the model does. The question is what you become when thinking is shared.

quotations_used.text: We have always been posthuman.
quotations_used.source: How We Became Posthuman, University of Chicago Press, 1999, p. 291

text_options.rationale: The vertical display text mirrors spread 018 exactly in structure, creating a visual bookend for the extensions sequence. 'When these models change, minds change' updates McLuhan's 'When these ratios change, men change.' The body text recapitulates the entire sequence in compressed form (wheel, book, electric, AI) before landing the climactic question. Hayles closes the sequence by naming the condition the reader has arrived at.

text_options.label: Friston convergence
text_options.display_text:
AI
circuitry,
an
extension
of
cognition
itself.
text_options.body_text:
An agent does not have a model of its world — it is a model.

If Friston is right — that cognition is Bayesian inference, that every living system persists by minimizing the gap between prediction and sensation — then the transformer does not simulate thought.

It performs thought.

On a different substrate. At a different scale. Without a body. Without mortality. Without any particular reason to stop.

quotations_used.text: An agent does not have a model of its world — it is a model.
quotations_used.source: Active Inference, MIT Press, 2022

quotations_used.text: We have always been posthuman.
quotations_used.source: How We Became Posthuman, University of Chicago Press, 1999, p. 291

text_options.rationale: Friston's line opens the body text as a kind of bomb. The conditional ('If Friston is right') maintains the book's intellectual honesty while making clear the implications are staggering. The four-word sentence 'It performs thought.' is the structural climax of M1. The list that follows (different substrate, scale, body, mortality) accelerates toward 'without any particular reason to stop' — which opens toward M2's acceleration theme.

text_options.label: The word 'itself'
text_options.display_text:
AI
circuitry,
an
extension
of
cognition



itself.
text_options.body_text:
The wheel extended the foot.
The book extended the eye.
Clothing extended the skin.
Electric circuitry extended the nervous system.

AI extends the thing that was doing the extending.

We have always been posthuman.

quotations_used.text: We have always been posthuman.
quotations_used.source: How We Became Posthuman, University of Chicago Press, 1999, p. 291

text_options.rationale: The display text separates 'itself' from the rest of the sentence with three blank lines of white space. The word falls down the page like the final step off a cliff. The body text compresses the entire extensions sequence into four lines, then names the recursion in one: 'AI extends the thing that was doing the extending.' This is the simplest possible statement of M1's climactic insight. Hayles closes as benediction, not argument.

design_specs.label: Convergence bookend

layout.description: Left page: vertical display text descending the page, echoing spread 018's layout precisely. Right page: the first AI-generated image in M1 — neural activation patterns flowing between biological and artificial substrates. The bookend structure (018's thesis, 025's climax) is visible to any reader who flips between them.
layout.left_page: Display text descending from top in the same typographic treatment as spread 018. Body text at bottom or mid-page. The Hayles quotation as closing line. The layout should be recognizably the same as 018, creating a structural rhyme.
layout.right_page: AI-generated image: abstract neural activation patterns. Left half of the image shows biological neural firing (organic, branching, warm). Right half shows artificial neural activation (geometric, gridded, cool). The two halves flow into each other at the center, sharing computational patterns across substrates.

typography.display_face: Helvetica Neue or Akzidenz-Grotesk — identical to spread 018
typography.body_face: Lyon Text
typography.special_treatment: If option C is chosen, 'itself' is set with dramatically increased leading — visually separated from the rest of the display text by several centimeters of white space, making it the gravitational center of the page.

image_direction.subject: Abstract visualization: biological neural activation on the left, flowing into artificial neural network activation on the right. The biological side is organic, branching, dendritic — resembling neural imaging. The artificial side is geometric, layered, structured — resembling network architecture diagrams. At the boundary, the two patterns merge, sharing structure. The image is the first AI-generated image in M1, marking the moment when the medium produces its own representation.
image_direction.bw_treatment: Black and white with a full grayscale range. The biological side warmer in tone (if possible within B&W constraints); the artificial side cooler. The convergence zone in medium gray. The overall effect should be more neuroscience visualization than digital art.

design_specs.rationale: The original culminated the extensions sequence with a radiating hub diagram and the line 'When these ratios change, men change.' This culminates with the recursion: cognition extending cognition. The structural bookend with 018 (same vertical layout, same typographic treatment) creates a felt symmetry. The shift from found images (019-024) to a generated image marks the precise moment when the extension produces its own representation — the medium IS the message at the level of image production. This is not a design convenience; it is a structural argument.
